cards.title: Mud Therapy — Principles & Physiological Effects

alerts.label: Definition
alerts.text: Mud therapy (Mrittikapachanam) uses wet earth / clay as a therapeutic agent. Its cooling, absorptive, anti-inflammatory, and detoxifying properties make it a fundamental naturopathic modality.

rows: High specific heat capacity of wet clay draws heat from body
Silica, magnesium, calcium, iron absorbed transdermally
Clay draws toxins, excess sebum, and waste products
Weight of mud pack applies gentle compression

pearls.label: 🟡 Ideal mud for therapy
pearls.text: Best therapeutic mud: black cotton soil or fullers earth (multani mitti) — high mineral content, fine texture. Should be collected from 3–4 feet depth (free of contaminants, pesticides, worms). Sun-dry, powder, sieve, and store in earthen pot. Reconstitute with clean water to a paste consistency (like soft dough) before application. Never use mud from near chemical factories, cremation grounds, or stagnant water sources.

cards._legacyHtml:
Definition
Mud therapy (Mrittikapachanam) uses wet earth / clay as a therapeutic agent. Its cooling, absorptive, anti-inflammatory, and detoxifying properties make it a fundamental naturopathic modality.
PropertyMechanismTherapeutic Effect
Heat absorptionHigh specific heat capacity of wet clay draws heat from bodyReduces local inflammation, fever, headache
Mineral contentSilica, magnesium, calcium, iron absorbed transdermallySkin nourishment, joint support
Absorptive actionClay draws toxins, excess sebum, and waste productsSkin detoxification, acne, psoriasis
Mechanical pressureWeight of mud pack applies gentle compressionReduces oedema, improves lymphatic drainage
Relaxant effectParasympathetic stimulation via skin receptorsMuscle relaxation, stress reduction
🟡 Ideal mud for therapy
Best therapeutic mud: black cotton soil or fullers earth (multani mitti) — high mineral content, fine texture. Should be collected from 3–4 feet depth (free of contaminants, pesticides, worms). Sun-dry, powder, sieve, and store in earthen pot. Reconstitute with clean water to a paste consistency (like soft dough) before application. Never use mud from near chemical factories, cremation grounds, or stagnant water sources.

cards.title: Mud Pack — Types & Application Technique

rows: Joint pain, varicose veins, local skin conditions

pearls.label: 🟡 Abdominal mud pack
pearls.text: Most commonly prescribed mud application in BNYS. Indication: constipation, IBS, indigestion, hyperacidity, colitis, liver disorders, obesity. Apply cool mud pack from navel to pubic symphysis, width of palm on either side. 20–30 minutes, morning before breakfast or 3 hours after meals. The cooling effect reduces intestinal inflammation, improves peristalsis, and calms vagal tone. Course: daily × 14 days for chronic conditions.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.text: — Wet mud to soft paste. Spread evenly on muslin cloth or directly on skin. Temperature: room temperature (cool) for inflammatory conditions; slightly warm for chronic stiffness.

steps.title: Application
steps.text: — Apply in smooth strokes in one direction. Cover with thin cloth or wet bandage to retain moisture. Patient should be comfortable and warm.

steps.title: Duration & removal
steps.text: — Remove before mud dries completely (retains heat once dry). Wipe with damp cloth, then warm water. Pat dry — do not rub.

steps.title: Post-treatment
steps.text: — Rest for 15–20 min. Avoid exposure to cold/wind. Offer warm herbal tea. Record skin response and patient feedback.

cards._legacyHtml:
TypeAreaMud thicknessDurationIndication
Mud pack — localAbdomen, eyes, head, back1.5–2 cm20–30 minConstipation, fever, headache, inflammation
Mud bath — full bodyEntire body (except head)1 cm all over20–45 minSkin diseases, arthritis, obesity, stress
Mud bath — partialArms, legs, or trunk only1 cm20–30 minJoint pain, varicose veins, local skin conditions
Mud compressSmall local area0.5–1 cm on cloth15–20 minSprains, bruises, swelling, insect bites
1
Preparation — Wet mud to soft paste. Spread evenly on muslin cloth or directly on skin. Temperature: room temperature (cool) for inflammatory conditions; slightly warm for chronic stiffness.
2
Application — Apply in smooth strokes in one direction. Cover with thin cloth or wet bandage to retain moisture. Patient should be comfortable and warm.
3
Duration & removal — Remove before mud dries completely (retains heat once dry). Wipe with damp cloth, then warm water. Pat dry — do not rub.
4
Post-treatment — Rest for 15–20 min. Avoid exposure to cold/wind. Offer warm herbal tea. Record skin response and patient feedback.
🟡 Abdominal mud pack
Most commonly prescribed mud application in BNYS. Indication: constipation, IBS, indigestion, hyperacidity, colitis, liver disorders, obesity. Apply cool mud pack from navel to pubic symphysis, width of palm on either side. 20–30 minutes, morning before breakfast or 3 hours after meals. The cooling effect reduces intestinal inflammation, improves peristalsis, and calms vagal tone. Course: daily × 14 days for chronic conditions.

cards.title: Eye Mud Pack

alerts.label: Indication
alerts.text: Eye strain, conjunctivitis (non-infective), headache from eye strain, refractive errors (adjunct), dark circles, periorbital puffiness, early cataract (adjunct).

alerts.label: Contraindications
alerts.text: Acute conjunctivitis (infective), corneal ulcer, open wounds around eyes, skin infections, known eye allergies, recent eye surgery.

pearls.label: 🟡 Course of treatment
pearls.text: For refractive errors and eye strain: daily morning application × 21 days, then alternate days × 1 month. Combine with netra tarpana (ghee eye bath in Ayurveda context), palming exercises, and screen time reduction. Never use mud with visible grit on eyes — filter through 3 layers of muslin before preparing eye packs. Refrigerated mud packs are more effective for conjunctivitis and headache.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.text: — Wet refined mud (no grit) to soft, smooth consistency. Shape into two oval pads (5×3 cm, 1 cm thick) on gauze strips.

steps.title: Position
steps.text: — Patient supine, eyes closed. Place mud pads gently over closed eyelids. No pressure on eyeball.

steps.title: Duration
steps.text: — 10–20 minutes. Monitor every 5 min for discomfort. Remove if patient feels pressure or irritation.

steps.title: Removal
steps.text: — Remove gently. Ask patient to keep eyes closed for 2 minutes. Splash cool clean water gently over closed eyes. Pat dry.

cards._legacyHtml:
Indication
Eye strain, conjunctivitis (non-infective), headache from eye strain, refractive errors (adjunct), dark circles, periorbital puffiness, early cataract (adjunct).
1
Preparation — Wet refined mud (no grit) to soft, smooth consistency. Shape into two oval pads (5×3 cm, 1 cm thick) on gauze strips.
2
Position — Patient supine, eyes closed. Place mud pads gently over closed eyelids. No pressure on eyeball.
3
Duration — 10–20 minutes. Monitor every 5 min for discomfort. Remove if patient feels pressure or irritation.
4
Removal — Remove gently. Ask patient to keep eyes closed for 2 minutes. Splash cool clean water gently over closed eyes. Pat dry.
Contraindications
Acute conjunctivitis (infective), corneal ulcer, open wounds around eyes, skin infections, known eye allergies, recent eye surgery.
🟡 Course of treatment
For refractive errors and eye strain: daily morning application × 21 days, then alternate days × 1 month. Combine with netra tarpana (ghee eye bath in Ayurveda context), palming exercises, and screen time reduction. Never use mud with visible grit on eyes — filter through 3 layers of muslin before preparing eye packs. Refrigerated mud packs are more effective for conjunctivitis and headache.

cards.title: Mud Therapy — Contraindications & Precautions

alerts.label: Precautions
alerts.text: Always check for clay/mineral allergies. Patch test 24hr before first application. Warm patient before and after in cold climates. Never apply over varicose veins with broken skin. Elderly and debilitated patients: shorter duration, constant monitoring.

rows: Infection risk — mud is not sterile
Full body mud bath may cause collapse
Circulatory stress from full body mud bath

cards._legacyHtml:
Absolute contraindications
ConditionReason
Open wounds, cuts, ulcersInfection risk — mud is not sterile
Active skin infections (fungal, bacterial)May spread infection
Severe eczema / psoriasis (acute)May irritate broken skin
High fever (>103°F / 39.4°C)Full body mud bath may cause collapse
Severe cardiac / renal failureCirculatory stress from full body mud bath
Pregnancy (abdominal application)Pressure contraindicated
Precautions
Always check for clay/mineral allergies. Patch test 24hr before first application. Warm patient before and after in cold climates. Never apply over varicose veins with broken skin. Elderly and debilitated patients: shorter duration, constant monitoring.

cards.title: Naturopathic Diet Therapy — Principles

alerts.label: Core BNYS principle
alerts.text: "Let food be thy medicine" — Hippocrates. Diet is the first and most fundamental therapeutic tool in naturopathy. All other treatments are secondary to correct nutrition.

rows: Unprocessed, unrefined, locally grown, seasonal foods carry highest vital energy (prana)
Reduce toxic load: avoid processed, preserved, refined, chemically treated foods
Diet tailored to constitution (prakriti), current condition (vikriti), digestion, and climate
70% alkaline-forming : 30% acid-forming ratio for optimal tissue health
Raw fruits and vegetables retain enzymes, vitamins, and vital force — cook minimally
Eat slowly, chew thoroughly (32 times per mouthful), in calm environment, with gratitude

pearls.label: 🟡 Alkaline vs acid forming foods
pearls.text: Alkaline-forming (eat freely): all fruits (even citrus — alkaline ASH), all vegetables, sprouts, millet, seeds, nuts (most), coconut water. Acid-forming (limit): meat, fish, eggs, dairy, refined grains, sugar, alcohol, coffee. Note: it is the ASH residue after metabolism — not the taste — that determines acid/alkaline effect. Lemon is alkaline-forming despite being acidic in taste.

Alkaline-forming (eat freely): all fruits (even citrus — alkaline ASH), all vegetables, sprouts, millet, seeds, nuts (most), coconut water. Acid-forming (limit): meat, fish, eggs, dairy, refined grains, sugar, alcohol, coffee. Note: it is the ASH residue after metabolism — not the taste — that determines acid/alkaline effect. Lemon is alkaline-forming despite being acidic in taste.

cards.title: Fasting Therapy — Types & Protocols

alerts.label: Fasting principle in naturopathy
alerts.text: Fasting is the most powerful natural tool for self-healing. It directs vital energy from digestion to elimination and repair. "The physician within" is activated by fasting.

rows: Detox, weight loss, skin diseases, digestive disorders
Milder detox, good for beginners, diabetics (with care)
Metabolic syndrome, obesity, insulin resistance, longevity
Digestive rest, spiritual, gut microbiome reset

pearls.label: 🟡 Healing crisis during fasting
pearls.text: Expect: headache (days 1–2), fatigue, irritability, bad breath, coated tongue, skin breakouts, body odour — these are signs of active detoxification, not side effects. Distinguish from dangerous symptoms: severe hypoglycaemia (trembling, confusion), cardiac palpitations, BP drop >20mmHg, vomiting blood. Never fast patients with T1DM, active TB, severe anaemia (Hb <8), eating disorders, or cachexia.

steps.title: Preparation (3 days pre-fast)
steps.text: — Gradually eliminate meat, dairy, cooked grains. Increase fruits and vegetables. Reduce portion size. Clear bowel with enema or laxative herbs.

steps.title: During fast
steps.text: — Rest, light yoga/walking only. Plenty of water/juice. Daily enema or colonic irrigation for fasts >3 days. Monitor BP, pulse, urine output, ketones.

steps.title: Breaking the fast (most critical)
steps.text: — Break with diluted fresh juice first, then small amount of fruit. Never break with solid food. Take same number of days as fasting to return to normal diet. Breaking wrongly can cause refeeding syndrome.

cards._legacyHtml:
Fasting principle in naturopathy
Fasting is the most powerful natural tool for self-healing. It directs vital energy from digestion to elimination and repair. "The physician within" is activated by fasting.
Fasting TypeDurationIntake allowedIndicationSupervision
Water fast1–3 days (short); 7–21 days (therapeutic)Water only (2–3L/day)Obesity, hypertension, chronic toxicity, spiritualMandatory >3 days
Juice fast3–21 daysFresh fruit/vegetable juices, coconut waterDetox, weight loss, skin diseases, digestive disordersRecommended
Fruit fast3–7 daysFresh fruits only (no bananas/dates initially)Milder detox, good for beginners, diabetics (with care)Self-supervised possible
Intermittent fast (16:8)OngoingAll food within 8-hour windowMetabolic syndrome, obesity, insulin resistance, longevitySelf-managed
Ekadashi fast2 days/month (lunar 11th)Fruits/water onlyDigestive rest, spiritual, gut microbiome resetSelf-managed
1
Preparation (3 days pre-fast) — Gradually eliminate meat, dairy, cooked grains. Increase fruits and vegetables. Reduce portion size. Clear bowel with enema or laxative herbs.
2
During fast — Rest, light yoga/walking only. Plenty of water/juice. Daily enema or colonic irrigation for fasts >3 days. Monitor BP, pulse, urine output, ketones.
3
Breaking the fast (most critical) — Break with diluted fresh juice first, then small amount of fruit. Never break with solid food. Take same number of days as fasting to return to normal diet. Breaking wrongly can cause refeeding syndrome.
🟡 Healing crisis during fasting
Expect: headache (days 1–2), fatigue, irritability, bad breath, coated tongue, skin breakouts, body odour — these are signs of active detoxification, not side effects. Distinguish from dangerous symptoms: severe hypoglycaemia (trembling, confusion), cardiac palpitations, BP drop >20mmHg, vomiting blood. Never fast patients with T1DM, active TB, severe anaemia (Hb <8), eating disorders, or cachexia.

cards.title: Fasting — Contraindications & Monitoring
cards._legacyHtml:
Absolute contraindications to therapeutic fasting
ConditionReason
Type 1 Diabetes MellitusRapid DKA risk without insulin
Active tuberculosisCaloric restriction worsens TB
Severe anaemia (Hb <8 g/dL)Further depletion risk
Cachexia / severe underweight (BMI <16)Life-threatening further loss
Pregnancy / breastfeedingFetal/infant nutrition requirements
Active eating disordersPsychological harm, refeeding risk
Recent surgery (<4 weeks)Healing requires adequate protein/calories
Severe cardiac failureElectrolyte imbalance risk
Weight check
Daily (expect 0.5–1 kg/day)
BP monitoring
Twice daily (orthostatic)
Urine output
Minimum 1L/day
Blood glucose
If diabetic: 4× daily

cards.title: Yoga Therapy Assessment — Initial Evaluation

alerts.label: BNYS yoga assessment principle
alerts.text: Yoga therapy in BNYS is therapeutic yoga — not fitness yoga. The goal is to select the right practice for the right patient at the right time. Assessment must precede prescription.

pearls.label: 🟡 Breath-holding time (BHT)
pearls.text: Simple clinical marker of respiratory and cardiovascular health. Normal: 25–40 seconds. <20 seconds = significant dysfunction (poor parasympathetic tone, CO₂ sensitivity, anxiety). >40 seconds = good cardiovascular fitness. Measure: normal exhale, then hold, time to first urge to breathe. Improves significantly with pranayama practice — a useful treatment outcome marker.

steps.title: Structural assessment
steps.text: — Posture (standing, sitting), spinal alignment (kyphosis, lordosis, scoliosis), joint ROM (shoulder, hip, spine), muscle tightness (hamstrings, hip flexors, thoracic), balance (single leg stand), gait.

steps.title: Breath assessment
steps.text: — Rate (normal 12–16/min), pattern (chest vs diaphragmatic), depth, smoothness, breath-holding capacity (BHT), any breathlessness at rest or exertion.

steps.title: Energy / vitality
steps.text: — Sleep quality, fatigue level (VAS), digestion, elimination pattern, stress levels (PSS score), mood (PHQ-9, GAD-7).

steps.title: Contraindication screen
steps.text: — Recent surgery, fractures, acute disc prolapse, severe HTN (>180/110), advanced glaucoma, pregnancy stage, cardiac history, dizziness/vertigo.

steps.title: Goal setting
steps.text: — What does the patient want to achieve? Pain relief, stress reduction, weight management, spiritual practice, disease management? Set realistic, measurable goals.

cards._legacyHtml:
BNYS yoga assessment principle
Yoga therapy in BNYS is therapeutic yoga — not fitness yoga. The goal is to select the right practice for the right patient at the right time. Assessment must precede prescription.
1
Structural assessment — Posture (standing, sitting), spinal alignment (kyphosis, lordosis, scoliosis), joint ROM (shoulder, hip, spine), muscle tightness (hamstrings, hip flexors, thoracic), balance (single leg stand), gait.
2
Breath assessment — Rate (normal 12–16/min), pattern (chest vs diaphragmatic), depth, smoothness, breath-holding capacity (BHT), any breathlessness at rest or exertion.
3
Energy / vitality — Sleep quality, fatigue level (VAS), digestion, elimination pattern, stress levels (PSS score), mood (PHQ-9, GAD-7).
4
Contraindication screen — Recent surgery, fractures, acute disc prolapse, severe HTN (>180/110), advanced glaucoma, pregnancy stage, cardiac history, dizziness/vertigo.
5
Goal setting — What does the patient want to achieve? Pain relief, stress reduction, weight management, spiritual practice, disease management? Set realistic, measurable goals.
🟡 Breath-holding time (BHT)
Simple clinical marker of respiratory and cardiovascular health. Normal: 25–40 seconds. <20 seconds = significant dysfunction (poor parasympathetic tone, CO₂ sensitivity, anxiety). >40 seconds = good cardiovascular fitness. Measure: normal exhale, then hold, time to first urge to breathe. Improves significantly with pranayama practice — a useful treatment outcome marker.

cards.title: Yoga Prescription by Condition

rows: Shavasana ★, Makarasana, seated forward bends, gentle inversions (Viparita Karani)
Nadi Shodhana ★, Bhramari, Ujjayi — slow rhythm
Kapalbhati, Bhastrika, strong inversions (Sirsasana), Surya Namaskar (vigorous)
Pavanamuktasana, Mandukasana, Dhanurasana, Ardha Matsyendrasana, Surya Namaskar (slow)
None specific — adjust intensity to fitness
Pawanmuktasana series, Cat-Cow, Balasana, Bhujangasana (gentle), Setu Bandhasana
Surya Namaskar (slow ×5), Ustrasana, Matsyasana, Viparita Karani, Shavasana ★, Yoga Nidra ★
Intense practices if highly anxious initially
Surya Namaskar ★ (build to 12 rounds), standing poses, Navasana, Ardha Chandrasana
None if no joint problems — modify for knees
Pursed lip breathing, Ujjayi ★, Anulom-Vilom — NEVER Kapalbhati in acute

pearls.label: 🟡 Yoga Nidra — therapeutic goldmine
pearls.text: Yoga Nidra (yogic sleep) is the single most powerful stress-reduction tool in BNYS — 30 minutes = 2–3 hours of deep sleep physiologically. Induces theta brainwave state (4–8 Hz). Clinical evidence: ↓ cortisol, ↓ sympathetic tone, ↑ DHEA, ↓ PTSD symptoms, ↓ chronic pain scores, ↓ diabetes-related distress. Prescription: 30 min daily, same time, supine. Start with guided recording (Bihar School of Yoga format). No contraindications except severe psychosis.

Type 2 DiabetesPavanamuktasana, Mandukasana, Dhanurasana, Ardha Matsyendrasana, Surya Namaskar (slow)Kapalbhati ★ (gentle), Bhastrika, Anulom-VilomNone specific — adjust intensity to fitness

Anxiety / DepressionSurya Namaskar (slow ×5), Ustrasana, Matsyasana, Viparita Karani, Shavasana ★, Yoga Nidra ★Bhramari ★, Nadi Shodhana, 4-7-8 breathingIntense practices if highly anxious initially

HypothyroidismSarvangasana (Shoulderstand) ★, Halasana, Matsyasana, Ujjayi, SimhasanaUjjayi ★, BhramariNone specific
🟡 Yoga Nidra — therapeutic goldmine
Yoga Nidra (yogic sleep) is the single most powerful stress-reduction tool in BNYS — 30 minutes = 2–3 hours of deep sleep physiologically. Induces theta brainwave state (4–8 Hz). Clinical evidence: ↓ cortisol, ↓ sympathetic tone, ↑ DHEA, ↓ PTSD symptoms, ↓ chronic pain scores, ↓ diabetes-related distress. Prescription: 30 min daily, same time, supine. Start with guided recording (Bihar School of Yoga format). No contraindications except severe psychosis.

cards.title: Pranayama — Clinical Guide

rows: Alternate nostril: 4 in left, 8 hold, 8 out right; reverse
↑ HRV, ANS balance, ↓ anxiety, ↑ cognitive function
↑ Metabolism, ↓ blood sugar, abdominal toning, expectorant
HTN (severe), epilepsy, pregnancy, hernia, acute asthma
Hum on exhale with fingers on face (Shanmukhi mudra)
↑ Nitric oxide, ↓ BP, vagal stimulation, calming
↑ O₂, energising, clears nadis, ↑ metabolic rate
HTN, cardiac disease, epilepsy, pregnancy, hernia
Inhale through curled tongue/teeth; exhale nose
Cooling, ↓ BP, ↓ pitta, thirst, hunger management

pearls.label: 🟡 Sequence of practice
pearls.text: Correct order: Asanas → Pranayama → Meditation/Yoga Nidra → Shavasana. Never reverse. Reason: asanas prepare the body and nervous system for pranayama; pranayama prepares the mind for meditation. Pranayama on an unprepared body (without asanas) can cause hyperventilation, dizziness, and emotional release. Minimum gap of 4 hours after meals for vigorous asanas; 2 hours for pranayama only.

cards._legacyHtml:
PranayamaTechniqueEffectDoseContraindication

KapalbhatiPassive inhalation, active sharp exhalation; 1/sec↑ Metabolism, ↓ blood sugar, abdominal toning, expectorant3–5 min; build slowlyHTN (severe), epilepsy, pregnancy, hernia, acute asthma
BhramariHum on exhale with fingers on face (Shanmukhi mudra)↑ Nitric oxide, ↓ BP, vagal stimulation, calming5–7 rounds, 2× dailySevere ear infection
BhastrikaForceful inhalation AND exhalation equally↑ O₂, energising, clears nadis, ↑ metabolic rate3 rounds × 10 breathsHTN, cardiac disease, epilepsy, pregnancy, hernia
UjjayiConstrict throat slightly, ocean-sound breath; 6–8/minCalming, ↑ HRV, ↑ endurance, thyroid stimulationThroughout yoga practice or 10 minNone

🟡 Sequence of practice
Correct order: Asanas → Pranayama → Meditation/Yoga Nidra → Shavasana. Never reverse. Reason: asanas prepare the body and nervous system for pranayama; pranayama prepares the mind for meditation. Pranayama on an unprepared body (without asanas) can cause hyperventilation, dizziness, and emotional release. Minimum gap of 4 hours after meals for vigorous asanas; 2 hours for pranayama only.

cards.title: Acupressure — Principles & Theory

alerts.label: Principle
alerts.text: Acupressure stimulates specific points (acupoints) on the body along energy channels (meridians) to restore the free flow of vital energy (Qi/Prana), correct imbalances, and activate the body's self-healing mechanisms.

rows: 14 main energy channels (12 organ meridians + 2 extraordinary). Each linked to specific organ systems.
365 classical points + extra points. Located at energy junctions with measurable electrical resistance differences.
Clockwise pressure = tonifies (strengthens). Anticlockwise = sedates (calms). Sustained pressure = balancing.
Hands, feet, and ears contain reflex maps of entire body. Stimulation affects corresponding organs.
Endorphin release, gate control pain theory, autonomic modulation, local micro-circulation improvement

pearls.label: 🟡 Deqi sensation
pearls.text: The therapeutic sensation at a correctly stimulated acupoint: dull ache, heaviness, tingling, warmth, or electric-like sensation radiating along the meridian. This is "Deqi" — means the Qi has arrived. It indicates correct point location and adequate stimulation. Absence of Deqi = incorrect location, insufficient pressure, or depleted Qi (chronic illness). Teach patients to recognise Deqi so they can do self-acupressure correctly.

cards._legacyHtml:
Principle
Acupressure stimulates specific points (acupoints) on the body along energy channels (meridians) to restore the free flow of vital energy (Qi/Prana), correct imbalances, and activate the body's self-healing mechanisms.
ConceptDescription
Meridians (Nadis)14 main energy channels (12 organ meridians + 2 extraordinary). Each linked to specific organ systems.
Acupoints365 classical points + extra points. Located at energy junctions with measurable electrical resistance differences.
Tonification vs SedationClockwise pressure = tonifies (strengthens). Anticlockwise = sedates (calms). Sustained pressure = balancing.
Zone therapy (reflexology)Hands, feet, and ears contain reflex maps of entire body. Stimulation affects corresponding organs.
Mechanism (modern)Endorphin release, gate control pain theory, autonomic modulation, local micro-circulation improvement
🟡 Deqi sensation
The therapeutic sensation at a correctly stimulated acupoint: dull ache, heaviness, tingling, warmth, or electric-like sensation radiating along the meridian. This is "Deqi" — means the Qi has arrived. It indicates correct point location and adequate stimulation. Absence of Deqi = incorrect location, insufficient pressure, or depleted Qi (chronic illness). Teach patients to recognise Deqi so they can do self-acupressure correctly.

cards.title: Key Acupressure Points — Clinical Reference

rows: 3 finger-widths above wrist crease, between tendons — inner forearm
Nausea ★ (CINV, morning sickness, travel), anxiety, palpitations, insomnia
4 finger-widths below kneecap, 1 finger lateral to tibial crest
Digestive disorders ★, fatigue, immune boost, knee pain, overall vitality tonic
Web between thumb and index finger — highest point of muscle belly
Headache ★, toothache, cold/flu, constipation, facial disorders, pain
Deep pinch pressure, 1–2 min each side
Base of skull, in hollow between SCM and trapezius muscles
Headache ★, neck pain, hypertension, cold/flu, dizziness, eye disorders
Firm upward pressure both sides simultaneously
4 finger-widths above medial malleolus, posterior to tibial border
Gynaecological disorders ★, insomnia, digestive issues, urinary problems
Firm pressure, 1–2 min. CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy
Centre of sole, in depression when toes curl — junction of anterior ⅓
Hypertension ★, insomnia, anxiety, grounding, kidney tonic
Firm pressure or rolling with thumb
Fatigue ★, digestive weakness, reproductive disorders, Qi deficiency
Top of head — midline, apex of skull
Headache, dizziness, mental clarity, prolapse conditions, depression
Gentle circular pressure — avoid vigorous in HTN

pearls.label: 🟡 Marma points (Ayurvedic)
pearls.text: 107 Marma points are vital energy junctions in Ayurveda — anatomically correspond to junctions of muscle, bone, ligament, vessel, and joint. Key clinical Marma points: Sthapani (between eyebrows — headache, stress), Talahridaya (palm/sole centre — cardiac, grounding), Brahmarandhra (crown — mental health), Kakshadhara (armpit — shoulder, lymph). Marma stimulation uses gentle pressure, rotation, and warm sesame/medicated oil. Contraindicated over open wounds, scars, or known tumours.

cards._legacyHtml:
PointLocationIndicationMethod
P6 (Neiguan)3 finger-widths above wrist crease, between tendons — inner forearmNausea ★ (CINV, morning sickness, travel), anxiety, palpitations, insomniaFirm circular pressure, 1–3 min
ST36 (Zusanli)4 finger-widths below kneecap, 1 finger lateral to tibial crestDigestive disorders ★, fatigue, immune boost, knee pain, overall vitality tonicDeep pressure + circular, 2–3 min
LI4 (Hegu)Web between thumb and index finger — highest point of muscle bellyHeadache ★, toothache, cold/flu, constipation, facial disorders, painDeep pinch pressure, 1–2 min each side
GB20 (Fengchi)Base of skull, in hollow between SCM and trapezius musclesHeadache ★, neck pain, hypertension, cold/flu, dizziness, eye disordersFirm upward pressure both sides simultaneously
SP6 (Sanyinjiao)4 finger-widths above medial malleolus, posterior to tibial borderGynaecological disorders ★, insomnia, digestive issues, urinary problemsFirm pressure, 1–2 min. CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy
KD1 (Yongquan)Centre of sole, in depression when toes curl — junction of anterior ⅓Hypertension ★, insomnia, anxiety, grounding, kidney tonicFirm pressure or rolling with thumb
CV6 (Qihai)1.5 cun below umbilicus, midlineFatigue ★, digestive weakness, reproductive disorders, Qi deficiencyGentle circular clockwise pressure
GV20 (Baihui)Top of head — midline, apex of skullHeadache, dizziness, mental clarity, prolapse conditions, depressionGentle circular pressure — avoid vigorous in HTN
🟡 Marma points (Ayurvedic)
107 Marma points are vital energy junctions in Ayurveda — anatomically correspond to junctions of muscle, bone, ligament, vessel, and joint. Key clinical Marma points: Sthapani (between eyebrows — headache, stress), Talahridaya (palm/sole centre — cardiac, grounding), Brahmarandhra (crown — mental health), Kakshadhara (armpit — shoulder, lymph). Marma stimulation uses gentle pressure, rotation, and warm sesame/medicated oil. Contraindicated over open wounds, scars, or known tumours.

cards.title: Foot Reflexology — Zone Map & Protocol

alerts.label: Theory
alerts.text: The feet contain a complete reflex map of the body. Pressure on specific reflex zones sends neurological signals to corresponding organs via spinal reflexes and autonomic pathways.

rows: Sciatica, menstrual disorders, low back pain
Entire spine (cervical at toe to lumbar at heel)

steps.title: Preparation
steps.text: — Warm foot bath 5 min. Pat dry. Apply sesame oil or reflexology cream. Patient supine or in reclining chair.

steps.title: General relaxation
steps.text: — 5 min: effleurage whole foot, ankle rotations, toe stretches, heel squeeze.

steps.title: Systematic pressure
steps.text: — Use thumb-walking technique on all zones. Note tender points (indicate congestion/dysfunction). Hold sensitive points with sustained pressure × 30 sec.

steps.title: Closing
steps.text: — General relaxation strokes. Solar plexus point (centre of ball) bilaterally with deep breath × 3. Duration: 30–45 min total.

cards._legacyHtml:
Theory
The feet contain a complete reflex map of the body. Pressure on specific reflex zones sends neurological signals to corresponding organs via spinal reflexes and autonomic pathways.
Foot ZoneCorresponding Organ/AreaClinical use
Big toe / toes 2–5Head, brain, sinuses, teethHeadache, sinusitis, eye/ear disorders
Ball of footHeart, lungs, chestRespiratory conditions, palpitations, anxiety
Upper archStomach, liver, gallbladder, pancreas, spleenDigestive disorders, diabetes, jaundice
Lower archSmall intestine, colon, kidneysIBS, constipation, UTI, kidney stones
HeelPelvis, sciatic nerve, reproductive organsSciatica, menstrual disorders, low back pain
Inner (medial) edgeEntire spine (cervical at toe to lumbar at heel)Spinal disorders, posture, back pain
Outer (lateral) edgeShoulder, elbow, knee, hip (down heel)Limb joint disorders
1
Preparation — Warm foot bath 5 min. Pat dry. Apply sesame oil or reflexology cream. Patient supine or in reclining chair.
2
General relaxation — 5 min: effleurage whole foot, ankle rotations, toe stretches, heel squeeze.
3
Systematic pressure — Use thumb-walking technique on all zones. Note tender points (indicate congestion/dysfunction). Hold sensitive points with sustained pressure × 30 sec.
4
Closing — General relaxation strokes. Solar plexus point (centre of ball) bilaterally with deep breath × 3. Duration: 30–45 min total.

cards.title: Massage Therapy in Naturopathy — Principles

alerts.label: BNYS massage principle
alerts.text: Naturopathic massage aims to restore circulation, lymphatic flow, nervous system balance, and tissue health — not merely relaxation. Oil selection is therapeutic, not aesthetic.

rows: Long gliding strokes, towards heart, full palm contact
Kneading, wringing, rolling of muscle tissue
↑ Local circulation, breaks adhesions, reduces muscle tension
Deep circular pressure on specific points/areas
Breaks scar tissue, resolves local adhesions
Fine trembling of hands on body part

pearls.label: 🟡 Therapeutic oils in BNYS
pearls.text: Sesame oil (tila taila): warming, anti-Vata, best for dry skin, joint disorders, neurological conditions — most universally used. Coconut oil: cooling, anti-inflammatory, best for Pitta, skin conditions, headaches. Mustard oil: deeply warming and stimulating — cold/flu, arthritis, cold constitutions. Neem oil: antimicrobial, antifungal — skin infections, psoriasis, eczema. Castor oil: deeply penetrating, anti-inflammatory — abdominal application, joint swelling. Always warm oil to body temperature before application.

cards._legacyHtml:
BNYS massage principle
Naturopathic massage aims to restore circulation, lymphatic flow, nervous system balance, and tissue health — not merely relaxation. Oil selection is therapeutic, not aesthetic.
Stroke TypeTechniqueEffectWhen used
EffleurageLong gliding strokes, towards heart, full palm contact↑ Venous/lymphatic return, relaxation, warm-upOpening and closing strokes
PetrissageKneading, wringing, rolling of muscle tissue↑ Local circulation, breaks adhesions, reduces muscle tensionMain body of massage
TapotementPercussion: hacking, cupping, tappingStimulates, loosens secretions (chest), energisesStimulating massage; chest therapy
FrictionDeep circular pressure on specific points/areasBreaks scar tissue, resolves local adhesionsSpecific tight areas, tendons
VibrationFine trembling of hands on body partLoosens secretions, nerve stimulation, relaxationChest, nerve pathways
🟡 Therapeutic oils in BNYS
Sesame oil (tila taila): warming, anti-Vata, best for dry skin, joint disorders, neurological conditions — most universally used. Coconut oil: cooling, anti-inflammatory, best for Pitta, skin conditions, headaches. Mustard oil: deeply warming and stimulating — cold/flu, arthritis, cold constitutions. Neem oil: antimicrobial, antifungal — skin infections, psoriasis, eczema. Castor oil: deeply penetrating, anti-inflammatory — abdominal application, joint swelling. Always warm oil to body temperature before application.

cards.title: Abhyanga — Full Body Oil Massage

alerts.label: Abhyanga (Ayurvedic oil massage)
alerts.text: Traditional whole-body massage with warm medicated oil. One of the most powerful rejuvenative (rasayana) therapies. 60–90 minute protocol.

pearls.label: 🟡 Self-abhyanga prescription
pearls.text: Daily self-abhyanga is one of the most powerful preventive health practices. Prescription: warm sesame oil, 10–15 min before morning shower. Start at crown, work down. Let oil absorb 5 min then shower with warm (not hot) water — do not use soap on oiled skin. Weekly self-abhyanga reduces Vata, improves sleep, ↓ cortisol, ↑ skin barrier function. Teach every chronically stressed patient this practice.

steps.title: Patient position & preparation
steps.text: — Warm room (no draught). Disrobe to underwear. Massage table with towels. Warm oil to 40–42°C. Begin supine.

steps.title: Head & scalp (10 min)
steps.text: — Circular pressure on scalp with fingertips. Stroke temples, occiput, forehead. Stimulates marma points: Adhipati (crown), Sthapani (forehead), Krikatika (nape).

steps.title: Face & neck (5 min)
steps.text: — Upward strokes on face. Long effleurage down neck towards clavicle (lymphatic drainage). Circular on jaw muscles.

steps.title: Chest & abdomen (10 min)
steps.text: — Effleurage chest outward. Abdomen: clockwise circular strokes following colon (ascending right → transverse → descending left).

steps.title: Arms & hands (10 min)
steps.text: — Long strokes from hand to shoulder. Circular at joints. Palm and finger massage. Reflex points on palm.

steps.title: Legs & feet (15 min)
steps.text: — Long effleurage from foot to hip. Circular at knee and ankle. Foot massage including heel and toe joints. Reflexology zones as indicated.

steps.title: Back (15 min)
steps.text: — Prone position. Long strokes from sacrum to neck. Circular on erector spinae. Shoulder blade region. Gluteal kneading.

steps.title: Steam bath (Svedana)
steps.text: — Follow with steam cabinet or wet sheet pack × 10–15 min to drive oil into tissues. Remove excess oil with dry towel or warm bath.

cards._legacyHtml:
Abhyanga (Ayurvedic oil massage)
Traditional whole-body massage with warm medicated oil. One of the most powerful rejuvenative (rasayana) therapies. 60–90 minute protocol.
1
Patient position & preparation — Warm room (no draught). Disrobe to underwear. Massage table with towels. Warm oil to 40–42°C. Begin supine.
2
Head & scalp (10 min) — Circular pressure on scalp with fingertips. Stroke temples, occiput, forehead. Stimulates marma points: Adhipati (crown), Sthapani (forehead), Krikatika (nape).
3
Face & neck (5 min) — Upward strokes on face. Long effleurage down neck towards clavicle (lymphatic drainage). Circular on jaw muscles.
4
Chest & abdomen (10 min) — Effleurage chest outward. Abdomen: clockwise circular strokes following colon (ascending right → transverse → descending left).
5
Arms & hands (10 min) — Long strokes from hand to shoulder. Circular at joints. Palm and finger massage. Reflex points on palm.
6
Legs & feet (15 min) — Long effleurage from foot to hip. Circular at knee and ankle. Foot massage including heel and toe joints. Reflexology zones as indicated.
7
Back (15 min) — Prone position. Long strokes from sacrum to neck. Circular on erector spinae. Shoulder blade region. Gluteal kneading.
8
Steam bath (Svedana) — Follow with steam cabinet or wet sheet pack × 10–15 min to drive oil into tissues. Remove excess oil with dry towel or warm bath.
🟡 Self-abhyanga prescription
Daily self-abhyanga is one of the most powerful preventive health practices. Prescription: warm sesame oil, 10–15 min before morning shower. Start at crown, work down. Let oil absorb 5 min then shower with warm (not hot) water — do not use soap on oiled skin. Weekly self-abhyanga reduces Vata, improves sleep, ↓ cortisol, ↑ skin barrier function. Teach every chronically stressed patient this practice.

cards.title: Massage Contraindications & Precautions

alerts.label: Precautions (modify, not contraindicate)
alerts.text: Varicose veins (massage above, not directly over). Pregnancy (avoid deep abdominal and lower back in 1st trimester; all massage after 1st trimester with appropriate positioning). Osteoporosis (gentle only — no deep pressure). Hypertension (avoid heavy tapotement). Diabetes (check skin integrity before and after, reduced sensation).

rows: Risk of embolism from dislodging clot
Risk of local spread (theoretical but cautious)
Acute inflammatory joint disease (gout attack, RA flare)

cards._legacyHtml:
Absolute contraindications
ConditionReason
Deep vein thrombosis (DVT)Risk of embolism from dislodging clot
Active infections (skin, systemic fever)Spreads infection; increases systemic load
Open wounds, burns, unhealed scarsDirect tissue damage risk
Active malignancy (over tumour site)Risk of local spread (theoretical but cautious)
Bleeding disorders / anticoagulantsBruising, haematoma risk
Acute fracturesMovement of fracture site
Acute inflammatory joint disease (gout attack, RA flare)Pain, worsens inflammation
Precautions (modify, not contraindicate)
Varicose veins (massage above, not directly over). Pregnancy (avoid deep abdominal and lower back in 1st trimester; all massage after 1st trimester with appropriate positioning). Osteoporosis (gentle only — no deep pressure). Hypertension (avoid heavy tapotement). Diabetes (check skin integrity before and after, reduced sensation).

cards.title: Counselling in Naturopathy — Core Principles

alerts.label: Role of counselling in BNYS
alerts.text: Health is physical, mental, social, and spiritual. A naturopathic physician must be able to identify psychological contributors to illness, provide basic supportive counselling, and refer appropriately. You cannot treat the body and ignore the mind.

rows: Full attention, no interruption, non-verbal engagement (nodding, eye contact, open posture)
Allow patient to complete their story before asking questions
Mirror back what was said in slightly different words
"So it sounds like you feel the stress at work is making your symptoms worse?"
Cannot be answered with yes/no — invite elaboration
"How has this been affecting your daily life?"
Acknowledge the emotional experience without judgment
"That sounds like it's been really difficult to manage alongside work."
Periodically recap to show understanding and check accuracy
"Let me just check I've understood correctly — you've had this for 3 months, worse with stress, better on weekends?"
Explore ambivalence; elicit patient's own motivation for change
"What would a healthier version of you look like in 6 months?"

pearls.label: 🟡 BATHE technique — 5 min psychosocial screen
pearls.text: B — Background: "What is going on in your life?" A — Affect: "How do you feel about that?" T — Trouble: "What troubles you most about it?" H — Handling: "How are you handling it?" E — Empathy: "That must be very difficult for you." This 5-question structure provides a complete psychosocial history in under 5 minutes and is proven to improve patient satisfaction and clinical outcomes. Use at every consultation.

cards._legacyHtml:
Role of counselling in BNYS
Health is physical, mental, social, and spiritual. A naturopathic physician must be able to identify psychological contributors to illness, provide basic supportive counselling, and refer appropriately. You cannot treat the body and ignore the mind.
SkillDescriptionExample
Active listeningFull attention, no interruption, non-verbal engagement (nodding, eye contact, open posture)Allow patient to complete their story before asking questions
ReflectionMirror back what was said in slightly different words"So it sounds like you feel the stress at work is making your symptoms worse?"
Open questionsCannot be answered with yes/no — invite elaboration"How has this been affecting your daily life?"
Empathy statementsAcknowledge the emotional experience without judgment"That sounds like it's been really difficult to manage alongside work."
SummarisingPeriodically recap to show understanding and check accuracy"Let me just check I've understood correctly — you've had this for 3 months, worse with stress, better on weekends?"
Motivational interviewingExplore ambivalence; elicit patient's own motivation for change"What would a healthier version of you look like in 6 months?"
🟡 BATHE technique — 5 min psychosocial screen
B — Background: "What is going on in your life?" A — Affect: "How do you feel about that?" T — Trouble: "What troubles you most about it?" H — Handling: "How are you handling it?" E — Empathy: "That must be very difficult for you." This 5-question structure provides a complete psychosocial history in under 5 minutes and is proven to improve patient satisfaction and clinical outcomes. Use at every consultation.

cards.title: Communication Skills — The Therapeutic Consultation

pearls.label: 🟡 The 90-second rule
pearls.text: Studies show doctors interrupt patients on average within 18 seconds of them beginning to speak. If allowed to speak uninterrupted, 80% of patients finish in under 90 seconds — and they reveal the real reason for the consultation. The first 90 seconds of a consultation should be the patient's time. Put down your pen. Look up from the screen. Just listen. This single change improves diagnosis accuracy, patient satisfaction, and adherence to treatment.

steps.title: Opening (2 min)
steps.text: — Warm greeting by name. Introduce yourself clearly. Check how patient wishes to be addressed. "How can I help you today?" — then LISTEN without interrupting for at least 90 seconds.

steps.title: Exploration (10–15 min)
steps.text: — ICE framework: Ideas ("What do you think might be causing this?"), Concerns ("What worries you most about it?"), Expectations ("What were you hoping I could do for you today?").

steps.title: Examination & assessment (5–10 min)
steps.text: — Explain what you're going to do before each step. Maintain dignity. Invite questions during examination.

steps.title: Shared decision-making (5 min)
steps.text: — Explain findings in lay language. Offer options, not prescriptions. "This is what I recommend, but ultimately you decide." Involve the patient in the treatment plan.

steps.title: Safety-netting & closing (2 min)
steps.text: — "If things don't improve in 2 weeks / if you experience X, please come back or seek urgent care." Written plan. Confirm understanding: "Is there anything you're unsure about?"

cards._legacyHtml:
1
Opening (2 min) — Warm greeting by name. Introduce yourself clearly. Check how patient wishes to be addressed. "How can I help you today?" — then LISTEN without interrupting for at least 90 seconds.
2
Exploration (10–15 min) — ICE framework: Ideas ("What do you think might be causing this?"), Concerns ("What worries you most about it?"), Expectations ("What were you hoping I could do for you today?").
3
Examination & assessment (5–10 min) — Explain what you're going to do before each step. Maintain dignity. Invite questions during examination.
4
Shared decision-making (5 min) — Explain findings in lay language. Offer options, not prescriptions. "This is what I recommend, but ultimately you decide." Involve the patient in the treatment plan.
5
Safety-netting & closing (2 min) — "If things don't improve in 2 weeks / if you experience X, please come back or seek urgent care." Written plan. Confirm understanding: "Is there anything you're unsure about?"
🟡 The 90-second rule
Studies show doctors interrupt patients on average within 18 seconds of them beginning to speak. If allowed to speak uninterrupted, 80% of patients finish in under 90 seconds — and they reveal the real reason for the consultation. The first 90 seconds of a consultation should be the patient's time. Put down your pen. Look up from the screen. Just listen. This single change improves diagnosis accuracy, patient satisfaction, and adherence to treatment.

cards.title: Mental Health Screening in Naturopathic Practice

alerts.label: Why screen in BNYS?
alerts.text: 70% of patients in naturopathic practice have a significant psychological component to their illness. PHQ-9 and GAD-7 take 2 minutes and are mandatory tools.

alerts.label: Mandatory referral indicators
alerts.text: PHQ-9 ≥20 or active suicidal ideation (Q9 score >0). Active psychosis or mania. Severe eating disorder (BMI <17.5 or rapid weight loss). Substance dependence. Any immediate safety concern. Always document referral and safety-netting in case notes.

rows: PHQ-9 ≥10 = discuss; ≥20 = REFER URGENTLY. Always ask Q9 (self-harm)
≥10 = moderate anxiety — consider referral if not improving
Intervene with sleep hygiene + yoga nidra + magnesium
High PSS → stress management programme priority

cards._legacyHtml:
Why screen in BNYS?
70% of patients in naturopathic practice have a significant psychological component to their illness. PHQ-9 and GAD-7 take 2 minutes and are mandatory tools.
ToolWhat it measuresScore interpretationAction
PHQ-9Depression (9 items, 0–27)0–4 minimal; 5–9 mild; 10–14 moderate; 15–19 mod-severe; 20–27 severePHQ-9 ≥10 = discuss; ≥20 = REFER URGENTLY. Always ask Q9 (self-harm)
GAD-7Generalised anxiety (7 items, 0–21)0–4 minimal; 5–9 mild; 10–14 moderate; 15–21 severe≥10 = moderate anxiety — consider referral if not improving
PSQISleep quality (7 domains)Score >5 = poor sleep qualityIntervene with sleep hygiene + yoga nidra + magnesium
PSS-10Perceived Stress Scale0–13 low; 14–26 moderate; 27–40 highHigh PSS → stress management programme priority
Mandatory referral indicators
PHQ-9 ≥20 or active suicidal ideation (Q9 score >0). Active psychosis or mania. Severe eating disorder (BMI <17.5 or rapid weight loss). Substance dependence. Any immediate safety concern. Always document referral and safety-netting in case notes.

cards.title: BNYS Casesheet — Structure & Format

alerts.label: Purpose of the casesheet
alerts.text: The BNYS casesheet is a comprehensive naturopathic history document. It integrates conventional biomedical history with naturopathic constitutional assessment — serving as both diagnostic and treatment planning tool.

rows: Name, age, sex, occupation, marital status, address, date, registration number
Patient's presenting problem in their own words + duration
SOCRATES: Site, Onset, Character, Radiation, Associations, Time/duration, Exacerbating/relieving factors, Severity (NRS 0–10)
Previous illnesses, hospitalisations, surgeries, accidents, chronic diseases, childhood illnesses
Hereditary conditions: diabetes, HTN, cancer, heart disease, thyroid, mental illness — in parents, siblings, children
Diet (type, habits, times, raw/cooked ratio), sleep (hours, quality, timing), bowel habits, urine output, exercise, occupation stress, habits (tobacco, alcohol)
Cycle regularity, flow, LMP, pregnancies, deliveries, contraception (female patients)
Current medications + dose + duration. Allergies. Previous naturopathic treatments.
Prakriti (Vata/Pitta/Kapha dominance), tongue examination, pulse (Nadi pariksha basics), iris/sclera observation
Systematic questioning: CVS, respiratory, GI, GU, MSK, neurological, skin, endocrine, mental health

cards._legacyHtml:
Purpose of the casesheet
The BNYS casesheet is a comprehensive naturopathic history document. It integrates conventional biomedical history with naturopathic constitutional assessment — serving as both diagnostic and treatment planning tool.
SectionContent
Patient identificationName, age, sex, occupation, marital status, address, date, registration number
Chief complaint (CC)Patient's presenting problem in their own words + duration
History of present illness (HPI)SOCRATES: Site, Onset, Character, Radiation, Associations, Time/duration, Exacerbating/relieving factors, Severity (NRS 0–10)
Past medical history (PMH)Previous illnesses, hospitalisations, surgeries, accidents, chronic diseases, childhood illnesses
Family history (FH)Hereditary conditions: diabetes, HTN, cancer, heart disease, thyroid, mental illness — in parents, siblings, children
Personal historyDiet (type, habits, times, raw/cooked ratio), sleep (hours, quality, timing), bowel habits, urine output, exercise, occupation stress, habits (tobacco, alcohol)
Menstrual/obstetric historyCycle regularity, flow, LMP, pregnancies, deliveries, contraception (female patients)
Drug historyCurrent medications + dose + duration. Allergies. Previous naturopathic treatments.
Naturopathic constitutional assessmentPrakriti (Vata/Pitta/Kapha dominance), tongue examination, pulse (Nadi pariksha basics), iris/sclera observation
Systems reviewSystematic questioning: CVS, respiratory, GI, GU, MSK, neurological, skin, endocrine, mental health

cards.title: BNYS Physical Examination — Naturopathic Approach

steps.title: General appearance
steps.text: — Nutritional status (obese/thin/normal), body type (ectomorph/mesomorph/endomorph), pallor, jaundice, cyanosis, clubbing, oedema, lymphadenopathy, hydration status

steps.title: Vital signs
steps.text: — BP (both arms), Pulse (rate, rhythm, volume, character), RR, Temperature (oral), SpO₂, Weight (kg), Height (cm), BMI, Waist circumference

steps.title: Tongue examination (naturopathic)
steps.text: — Colour (pale=deficiency, red=heat, purple=stasis), coating (white=cold/dampness, yellow=heat, thick=excess), shape (swollen=dampness, thin=deficiency), moisture, cracks, teeth marks

steps.title: Skin & nails
steps.text: — Texture (dry/oily), turgor, elasticity, rashes, discolouration, nail changes (koilonychia=iron def, leukonychia, Beau's lines, clubbing)

steps.title: Abdominal examination
steps.text: — Inspection (distension, visible peristalsis), palpation (tenderness, guarding, organomegaly), percussion (tympany, dullness), auscultation (bowel sounds)

steps.title: Naturopathic-specific assessment
steps.text: — Spinal palpation (vertebral tenderness, kyphosis/lordosis/scoliosis), reflex zone tenderness (feet/hands), muscle tension patterns, breathing pattern (chest vs diaphragmatic)

cards._legacyHtml:
1
General appearance — Nutritional status (obese/thin/normal), body type (ectomorph/mesomorph/endomorph), pallor, jaundice, cyanosis, clubbing, oedema, lymphadenopathy, hydration status
2
Vital signs — BP (both arms), Pulse (rate, rhythm, volume, character), RR, Temperature (oral), SpO₂, Weight (kg), Height (cm), BMI, Waist circumference
3
Tongue examination (naturopathic) — Colour (pale=deficiency, red=heat, purple=stasis), coating (white=cold/dampness, yellow=heat, thick=excess), shape (swollen=dampness, thin=deficiency), moisture, cracks, teeth marks
4
Skin & nails — Texture (dry/oily), turgor, elasticity, rashes, discolouration, nail changes (koilonychia=iron def, leukonychia, Beau's lines, clubbing)
5
Abdominal examination — Inspection (distension, visible peristalsis), palpation (tenderness, guarding, organomegaly), percussion (tympany, dullness), auscultation (bowel sounds)
6
Naturopathic-specific assessment — Spinal palpation (vertebral tenderness, kyphosis/lordosis/scoliosis), reflex zone tenderness (feet/hands), muscle tension patterns, breathing pattern (chest vs diaphragmatic)

cards.title: SOAP Note — BNYS Format

pearls.label: 🟡 Documentation principle
pearls.text: "If it isn't written, it didn't happen." Good documentation protects the patient (continuity of care), protects you (medico-legal), and improves outcomes (structured thinking leads to better plans). Write legibly in black ink. Date and sign every entry. Never use correction fluid — draw a single line through errors and initial. Document patient consent for all treatments. BNYS case notes should be kept for minimum 7 years.

steps.title: Subjective
steps.text:
CC: [Chief complaint] × [duration]
        HPI: [SOCRATES summary]
        PMH / FH / DH / SH: [Relevant positives and negatives]
        Diet: [Type, times, habits, water intake, raw/cooked]
        Sleep: [Hours, quality, timing]
        Bowels: [Frequency, consistency, colour]
        Stress: [Level 0–10, sources]
        Exercise: [Type, frequency, duration]

        6. Lifestyle: [Sleep, stress, exercise modifications]

4. Yoga/pranayama: [Specific prescription — asanas, pranayama, duration]
5. Supplements/herbs: [Name, dose, form, duration, timing]
6. Lifestyle: [Sleep, stress, exercise modifications]
7. Patient education: [Topics covered]
8. Safety-netting: [When to return / seek urgent care]
9. Review: [Date + objectives to reassess]
🟡 Documentation principle
"If it isn't written, it didn't happen." Good documentation protects the patient (continuity of care), protects you (medico-legal), and improves outcomes (structured thinking leads to better plans). Write legibly in black ink. Date and sign every entry. Never use correction fluid — draw a single line through errors and initial. Document patient consent for all treatments. BNYS case notes should be kept for minimum 7 years.

cards.title: Treatment Progress Notes & Discharge Summary

alerts.label: Discharge summary must include
alerts.text:
Admission and discharge dates
        Diagnosis at admission and discharge
        All treatments given (with dates, duration, response)
        Outcome measures at admission vs discharge
        Home programme prescribed (diet, yoga, self-care)
        Follow-up plan and date
        When to seek emergency care (safety-netting)
        Referring/treating doctor signature + date

steps.title: Date & visit number
steps.text: — e.g., "Day 7 of 21-day residential programme"

steps.title: Interval history
steps.text: — "Since last visit: [symptom change, treatment response, adherence, any adverse reactions, new complaints]"

steps.title: Repeat objective measures
steps.text: — Vitals, weight, relevant exam findings, outcome score changes

steps.title: Assessment update
steps.text: — "Responding well / partial response / no response / worsening — possible reasons:"

steps.title: Plan modification
steps.text: — Continue / escalate / modify / refer

cards._legacyHtml:
Progress note format (each visit)
1
Date & visit number — e.g., "Day 7 of 21-day residential programme"
2
Interval history — "Since last visit: [symptom change, treatment response, adherence, any adverse reactions, new complaints]"
3
Repeat objective measures — Vitals, weight, relevant exam findings, outcome score changes
4
Assessment update — "Responding well / partial response / no response / worsening — possible reasons:"
5
Plan modification — Continue / escalate / modify / refer
Discharge summary must include
Admission and discharge dates
Diagnosis at admission and discharge
All treatments given (with dates, duration, response)
Outcome measures at admission vs discharge
Home programme prescribed (diet, yoga, self-care)
Follow-up plan and date
When to seek emergency care (safety-netting)
Referring/treating doctor signature + date
